# Act 1: The Problem {.section}

---

## 01. The Golden Circle

<p class="slide-kicker">Direction of thinking and communicating</p>

<div class="bpm-compare">
<div class="bpm-flow muted">
<div class="bpm-lane">Default communication</div>
<div class="bpm-steps">
<div class="bpm-step">What we build</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">How it works</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step weak">Why it matters</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="bpm-flow accent">
<div class="bpm-lane">Golden Circle communication</div>
<div class="bpm-steps">
<div class="bpm-step primary">Why it matters</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">How we make it true</div>
<div class="bpm-arrow">-></div>
<div class="bpm-step">What we build</div>
</div>
</div>
</div>

<div class="references">
Reference: Simon Sinek, <a href="https://www.ted.com/talks/simon_sinek_how_great_leaders_inspire_action">How great leaders inspire action</a>, TEDxPuget Sound.
</div>

<aside class="notes">
This deck follows the Golden Circle deliberately. First establish the failure
mode Protopipe exists to address, then the operating model, then the platform
components.
</aside>

---

## 02. Why This Matters for Protopipe

<div class="circle-stack">
<div class="circle-item why">
<strong>Why</strong>

The failure we are unwilling to accept.
</div>

<div class="circle-item how">
<strong>How</strong>

The operating model that makes the failure preventable.
</div>

<div class="circle-item what">
<strong>What</strong>

The platform that makes the model tangible.
</div>
</div>

<aside class="notes">
Use this slide to explain the communication purpose: the audience should not
evaluate Protopipe as a feature list. They should first understand the failure
mode, then the operating model, then the product surface.
</aside>

---

## 03. Protopipe's Golden Circle

<div class="golden-circle">
<table>
<thead>
<tr>
<th>Why</th>
<th>How</th>
<th>What</th>
</tr>
</thead>
<tbody>
<tr>
<td>What failure are we unwilling to accept?</td>
<td>What operating model makes the failure preventable?</td>
<td>What platform makes the model tangible?</td>
</tr>
<tr>
<td>Missing feedback, invisible dependencies, opinion-driven decisions.</td>
<td>Shared language, measurable hypotheses, observable responsibility.</td>
<td>An Augmented Product Development Platform.</td>
</tr>
</tbody>
</table>
</div>

---

## 04. Core Thesis

Organizations do not primarily fail because of bad people, bad tools, or bad intentions.

They fail because decisions, dependencies, and discussions are not connected to measurable reality.

<div class="problem-triad">
<div class="problem">
<strong>Decisions without measurable feedback</strong>

Strategy becomes belief when impact cannot be observed.
</div>

<div class="problem">
<strong>Invisible dependencies</strong>

Architecture becomes risk when change propagation is hidden.
</div>

<div class="problem">
<strong>Discussions that produce opinions instead of responsibility</strong>

Communication becomes theater when nobody owns observable consequences.
</div>
</div>

<aside class="notes">
This is the first real content slide. Keep it sharp and let the rest of the deck
unpack these three claims.
</aside>

---

## 05. Modern Organizations Do Not Share One Reality

<div class="toc-only">
CFO, CTO, Product, Sales, Operations, and Developers each operate with different
evidence, cadences, incentives, and risk models.
</div>

---

## 06. Local Optimization Creates Organizational Drift

<div class="toc-only">
Each function can make rational decisions locally while the organization drifts
globally.
</div>

---

## 07. Luhmann: Organizations Are Communication Systems

<div class="toc-only">
Use Luhmann to explain why communication, not intent, becomes the operating
material of the organization.
</div>

---

## 08. Habermas: Decisions Need Shared Understanding

<div class="toc-only">
Use Habermas to connect deliberation, evidence, legitimacy, and mutual
understanding.
</div>

---

## 09. The Missing Conditions

<div class="toc-only">
Organizations lack shared language, shared evidence, and shared memory.
</div>

---

## 10. Problem Taxonomy

<div class="toc-only">
Map Noesis problem categories to the enterprise crisis: decision-making,
organization and process, culture and mental models, architecture and systems,
learning and product, metrics and steering.
</div>
